reasoning:
The current BBB OAS is sitting at 1.10% (as of June 20, 2025). This compares to its all-time cyclical lows near 1.00% around late 2024 and early 2025, and a recent high of 1.46% in April 2025. In the short term (7 days to 1 month), the spread has been extremely flat, resting exactly at 1.10% for consecutive days. Because the resolution criteria asks whether the spread will have strictly *increased*, flat readings (equality) resolve to NO. Therefore, short horizon probabilities must be marked down below 50% to account for the substantial chance of the value remaining exactly 1.10%. 

As the horizon extends (3 to 6 months), the probability distribution flattens. Given that at 1.10%, the BBB OAS is historically quite low, mean reversion suggests the risk is asymmetric to the upside over a longer timeframe (since spreads have a hard floor around ~0.95%–1.00% but can spike significantly wider on credit concerns, as seen in April 2025). This asymmetry pushes long-term expectations upward relative to random walk symmetry. To comply with increasing uncertainty across horizons (closing in on maximum uncertainty near 0.50), the probabilities shift smoothly from <50% (driven by short-term equality logic) to near 50% for 3 and 6-month horizons, where the random-walk equality risk diminishes and fundamentals balance out.
